did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning algorithms, specifically the LeNet classifier and Wide ResNet, to perform skin cancer detection and classification on dermoscopy images from the ISIC dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results comparing the LeNet classifier and Wide ResNet algorithm for skin cancer detection using the ISIC dataset. It includes specific methodology (data splitting, training/testing protocols), quantitative results (accuracy of 95.56% vs 92.95%, p-values, confidence intervals), and statistical analysis (t-tests), which are characteristic of a primary research study rather than a review.

title: Skin Cancer Detection using LeNet Classifier in Comparison with Wide ResNet Algorithm
journal: Proceedings of the 7th International Conference on Intelligent Sustainable Systems (ICISS-2025)
brief_summary: This study compares the LeNet classifier and Wide ResNet algorithm for skin cancer detection using dermoscopy images from the ISIC dataset, accessed via Kaggle. The dataset of 1,638 samples was split into 70% for training, 15% for testing, and 15% for validation. The LeNet model achieved a mean accuracy of 95.56%, significantly outperforming Wide ResNet's 92.95% accuracy. Statistical analysis using an independent sample t-test confirmed this difference was significant (p=0.000). The results suggest that the lightweight LeNet architecture is more effective for this specific task than the more complex Wide ResNet.

lesion_types_studied.details: Included in the dataset gathered from the ISIC dataset via Kaggle. The paper notes that the dataset was split into an equal number of photos for Group 1 and Group 2, with the exception of moles and melanomas, implying these were key classes in the source data, though the specific binary or multi-class breakdown used for the final LeNet vs Wide ResNet comparison is not explicitly detailed beyond the general 'skin cancer' label.

Mentioned as a class present in the ISIC dataset ('exception of moles and melanomas'). The system is described as determining risk for 'nevus' in the context of the expert system's output categories (high, low, or medium risk).

The primary classification task is 'skin cancer detection' and 'diagnosis'. The models classify images as 'skin cancer' versus presumably normal or benign, or among malignant types, but the specific subset of the ISIC dataset (e.g., BCC, SCC) used for the 1,638 samples is not explicitly enumerated beyond the general reference to melanoma and moles in the dataset description.
Mentioned in the Introduction as one of the three most prevalent forms of skin cancer, but not explicitly stated as a specific class label used in the experimental dataset or model training.
Mentioned in the Introduction as one of the three most prevalent forms of skin cancer, but not explicitly stated as a specific class label used in the experimental dataset or model training.

all_task_types.details: The paper performs skin cancer detection and classification using LeNet and Wide ResNet classifiers on dermoscopy images. The models predict discrete diagnostic categories (skin cancer vs. normal/benign or specific lesion types like melanoma/moles) and output accuracy metrics (95.56% for LeNet, 92.95% for Wide ResNet). The system also categorizes risk levels (high, low, medium) for nevus, which is a classification task.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Dataset (via Kaggle)
datasets_used.description: The International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset, gathered via the open-access website Kaggle. The study notes that subsets were split into an equal number of photos, with the exception of moles and melanomas.

use.training: 70% of the images (1146 samples) were used as training data.
use.validation: 15% of the images (245 samples) were used for validation.
use.testing: 15% of the images (245 samples) were used for testing.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Levene's test for equality of variances

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the mean accuracy of the LeNet classifier and the Wide ResNet classifier to determine if the difference in performance was statistically significant.

statistical_methods_used.method: Levene's test for equality of variances
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the assumption of homogeneity of variances prior to conducting the independent sample t-test.
To estimate the range of the mean difference between the two classifiers' accuracies.

statistical_methods_used.method: Descriptive Statistics (Mean, Standard Deviation, Standard Error)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To summarize the distribution of accuracy values for both the LeNet and Wide ResNet models across 20 trials.
To determine the required sample size for the study, using a power of 0.85 and an alpha level of 0.05.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in the mean accuracy between the LeNet classifier and the Wide ResNet classifier.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in the mean accuracy between the LeNet classifier and the Wide ResNet classifier (specifically, LeNet has higher accuracy).
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The independent sample t-test yielded a p-value of 0.000, which is below the significance level of 0.05. This indicates that the difference in mean accuracy between LeNet (95.56%) and Wide ResNet (92.95%) is statistically significant, leading to the conclusion that LeNet is a better model for detecting skin cancer.

ISIC Dataset (via Kaggle).metric: Accuracy (Mean, Standard Deviation, Standard Error) over 20 trials

statistical_comparison.interpretation: LeNet significantly outperforms Wide ResNet in accuracy.

ISIC Dataset (via Kaggle).notes: The application's overall accuracy rate was reported as 91% in the expert system context, but the specific model comparison results are 95.56% for LeNet and 92.95% for Wide ResNet. The dataset consisted of 1,638 samples split into 70% training, 15% testing, and 15% validation. The comparison was based on 20 trials for each model.

LeNet.description: Convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture created in the 1990s by Yann LeCun, Leon Bottou, Yoshua Bengio, and Patrick Haffner for recognizing handwritten characters.
LeNet.layers: Seven layers total: two convolutional layers, two subsampling (pooling) levels, and three fully connected layers.
LeNet.components: Convolutional layers, pooling layers, fully connected layers.

Wide ResNet.description: Deep neural network architecture created to enhance ResNet performance for very deep networks, utilizing broader convolutional layers than standard ResNet.
Wide ResNet.components: Residual blocks (made up of two convolutional layers and a shortcut connection), broader convolutional layers.

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly reported (mentions Adam or stochastic gradient descent as examples of optimizers used in LeNet compilation generally, but not specific to this experiment)

classification_or_output_head.risk_levels: High, low, or medium risk for nevus (in the context of the expert system application)

preprocessing_techniques: Dataset splitting into training (70%), testing (15%), and validation (15%) sets
conclusions: The LeNet classifier achieved a mean accuracy of 95.56% for skin cancer detection, significantly outperforming the Wide ResNet algorithm, which achieved a mean accuracy of 92.95%.

Statistical analysis using an independent sample t-test confirmed the performance difference was significant (p=0.000), indicating that LeNet is a more effective model for this specific task than Wide ResNet.

The study concludes that the choice of deep learning model significantly influences accuracy in dermatological diagnosis, with the simpler, lightweight LeNet architecture being better suited for handling dermoscopy image data than the more complex Wide ResNet.
The authors acknowledge that a primary limitation of current methods is the difficulty of early skin cancer detection and the unpleasant, time-consuming nature of traditional biopsy samples.
Future work aims to expand the system to accommodate more items and lower training time, potentially through further optimization of LeNet or hybrid approaches combining the strengths of both models.
important_remarks: The study uses a very small dataset (1,638 samples) sourced from Kaggle, which may limit the generalizability of the results compared to larger, multi-center clinical datasets.

The paper does not report standard clinical metrics such as sensitivity, specificity, precision, or F1-score, relying solely on accuracy, which is insufficient for evaluating diagnostic performance in imbalanced medical datasets.

The dataset composition is vaguely described as having 'equal number of photos' for subsets with 'exception of moles and melanomas', raising concerns about class imbalance and lack of transparency in ground truth labels.

There is a potential data leakage risk as the paper does not explicitly state whether patient-level or image-level splitting was used; if images from the same patient appear in both training and testing sets, performance estimates would be inflated.

The comparison between LeNet (a 1990s architecture) and Wide ResNet is considered outdated; LeNet is generally too simple for modern dermoscopy tasks, making the conclusion that it 'outperforms' Wide ResNet potentially misleading due to overfitting on a small dataset rather than genuine superiority.
The study lacks external validation on independent datasets, relying only on a single internal split (70/15/15), which limits the robustness of the findings.
The paper mentions an 'expert system' with 91% accuracy but does not clarify if this refers to the same models or a different implementation, creating ambiguity in the reported results.

No information is provided regarding the computational efficiency (inference time, FLOPs, parameter count) despite the claim that LeNet is 'lightweight', making it difficult to assess its practical deployment benefits over Wide ResNet.
